content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 15, 2006


Carol Cowley
City Clerk
City of La Puente
15900 E. Main Street
La Puente, CA 91744-4719

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-05-227

Dear Ms. Cowley:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Councilmember John Solis and the City of La Puente, regarding the mass mail provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because the facts presented are of a general nature and do not fully identify the specific facts involved in the governmental decision at issue, we are treating your request as a request for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

     Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

     Please note that there are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict of interest provisions, which may apply to your situation. Laws outside the Act which may be implicated in the design and publication of the newspaper include, but are not limited to, sections 1090 et seq. regarding contracts, as well as section 8314 and Penal Code section 424 which proscribe the use of public moneys for campaign-related activities by state and local governmental entities. (See also Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal.3d 206.)

_______________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All further references to statutory "sections" will be to the Government Code and all further references to "regulations" will be to title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-05-227
Page 2

QUESTION

May Councilmember Solis, or non-incumbent candidate for that office, purchase
advertising space in the City of La Puente's community newspaper for political purposes
without implicating the mass mailing provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION

The prohibitions against publicly funded mass mailings described in the Act apply
to incumbent elected officials.

FACTS

The City of La Puente (the "City") publishes a free monthly community
newspaper, the Spotlight, the cost of which is paid for out of the General Fund. The City
does allow businesses and other individuals to buy advertising to offset the cost of
printing, publishing and distributing the newspaper. The City has received a request to
allow candidates to purchase space to run campaign ads in the Spotlight (the
"newspaper").

In phone calls with Commission counsel on December 12 and 14, 2005, and
February 7, 2006, you provided additional information regarding your question. You
indicated that the requestor is Councilmember John Solis. You also indicated that this
free newspaper is distributed by a newspaper carrier, and through the mail, to
approximately 1,100 households and businesses, i.e., all dwelling units in the City. The
Spotlight is not an adjudicated general circulation newspaper. The city council granted
the City's staff authority to create and maintain this newspaper. Though you do not know
how the original list of subscribers' addresses was created, you indicated that, as a free
publication, the City distributes copies of the newspaper on its own accord and not in
response to unsolicited requests by residents.

Additionally, you indicated that distribution is at least partially paid for with
General Fund money and that over $50 of General Fund money is spent on design,
production, and printing per issue. Finally, you indicated that councilmember Solis
contemplates paying the going rate for advertising space that the City charges any other
purchaser of ad space in the Spotlight.

ANALYSIS

A. The Act's Pertinent Mass Mailing Statute And Regulation

In 1988, section 89001 of the Act was added to provide that "[n]o newsletter or
other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." In order to avoid the absurd result of
literally prohibiting any and all mass mailings created or distributed with public funds,

File No. I-05-227
Page 3

regardless of their content or purpose, the Commission adopted regulation 18901,
clarifying which mailings were permissible and which were not.

Under regulation 18901, a mass mailing is defined as more than 200 substantially
similar tangible items delivered in a calendar month, by any means, to recipients at their
residence, place of employment, business, or post office box. (See also section 82041.5
and Reg. 18435 [defining "mass mailing"].) The Act's key provision governing mass
mailings is regulation 18901, subdivision (a), which states:

"(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), a mailing is prohibited by section
89001 if all of the following criteria are met:

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes of
this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

"(2) The item sent either:

"(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the mailing, or

"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer.

"(3) (A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or

"(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $ 50.00 are paid
with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is done with
the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation.

"(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single
calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any item described in subdivision (b)."

In order for an item to be a prohibited "mass mailing" as defined under the Act,
all four, enumerated criteria listed above must be met. (See Reg. 18901, subds.(a)(1)–
(a)(4).) If all four criteria under subdivision (a) apply, the mass mailing shall be
prohibited, barring the application of any exceptions. (See e.g., Reg. 18901, subds.(b)(1)
– (b)(11) [listing the general exceptions to the Act's mass mailing prohibition].)

File No. I-05-227
Page 4

B. Transmission Of A "Tangible" Item

Subdivision (a)(1) of regulation 18901, only restricts items that are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's home, office or post office box. If items are set
out for the public to pick up on their own, or are handed out in a public area, the
restrictions of the regulation do not apply. You indicated that approximately 1,100
subscribers receive the newspaper from either a newspaper carrier or through the mail.
Therefore, the criterion in subdivision (a)(1) is met.

C. "Elected Officer"

The second of the four criteria under regulation 18901, subdivision (a), indicates
that if reference is made to an "elected officer," the contemplated mailing could be
prohibited. (Reg. 18901(a)(2).)

According to the Act, an "elected officer" is any person who holds an elective
office, has been elected to an elective office but has not yet taken office, or who is
appointed to fill a vacant elective office. (Section 82020.) "Elective office" means any
state, regional, county, municipal, district, or judicial office which is filled at an election,
as well as membership on a county central committee of a qualified political party, and
members elected to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees' Retirement
System (PERS). (Section 82023.)

Therefore, since city council members are "elected officers," they (including Mr.
Solis) meet this criterion. But candidates for city council who have not yet been elected
to office, and are not otherwise elected officers affiliated with the City, are not subject to
the mass mailing restrictions of the Act.

D. References To Elected Officers Affiliated With The Agency

Subdivision (a)(2) of regulation 18901, generally describes two categories of
mass mailings which are regulated under the Act: (A) items that feature an elected officer
affiliated with the agency producing or sending the mailing, and (B) items that don't
necessarily feature, but include, a reference to an elected officer affiliated with the
agency and are prepared or sent in cooperation with that elected officer. You should be
aware that these two general restrictions have some exceptions found in subdivision (b)
of regulation 18901.

Regardless, a mass mailing which does not include any reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the mailing is not subject to
the restrictions of the regulation. (Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2); Dale Advice Letter, No. A-02-
331.) An "[e]lected officer affiliated with the agency" is defined as an elected officer
who is a "member, officer, or employee of the agency, or of a subunit thereof such as a
committee, or who has supervisory control over the agency, or who appoints one or more
members of the agency." (Reg. 18901, subd.(c)(1).)

File No. 1-05-227
Page 5

1. Items Featuring An Elected Officer

If a mass mailing "features" an elected officer, it is subject to regulation 18901.
(See Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2)(A).) A mass mailing "[f]eatures an elected officer" if it
includes the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the elected officer by
the manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the document, such as
by headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color. (Reg. 18901, subd.(c)(2); see
also Reg. 18901, subd. (b)(9)(B).)

Therefore, any tangible mass mailing item including any photographs of an
elected officer affiliated with the agency is prohibited. (Reg. 18901, susds.(a)(2)(A),
(b)(9)(B) and (c)(2).)

2. Items Referencing An Elected Officer, The Preparation Or
Sending Of Which Is Accomplished In Cooperation With The Official

If a mass mailing merely includes a reference to, but does not necessarily feature,
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected
officer, it is subject to regulation 18901. (Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2)(B).) A mass mailing
references an elected official when it includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to that person. (Ibid.)

E. Public Money

Pursuant to subdivision (a)(3), the "public money" criterion of the regulation is
met if: "(A) Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys; or [¶] (B)
Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $ 50.00 are paid with public moneys,
and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other
than as permitted by this regulation."

Even if a newspaper is only partially funded with public money, the mailing must
comply with section 89001. (Moore Advice Letter, No. A-99-234; Cf. Curtis Advice
Letter, No.: A-94-053.) This means that even if part of the publication of the newspaper
is funded through money received from those purchasing advertising space, the "public
money" criterion is met if any of the costs of the newspaper's distribution is paid for with
public funds, or if over $50 of design, production, or printing costs are paid for with
public funds. (See Houston Advice Letter, No. 1-05-183; Schetcman Advice Letter, No.
1-03-233.)

With specific regard to advertisements in publicly-funded publications, the
Houston Advice Letter, No. A-04-026, states that the prohibitions against mass mailings
at public expense would apply to an advertisement placed and paid for by a city
councilmember for his accounting firm in a chamber of commerce newsletter that was
funded in part by city funds. On the other hand, such prohibitions do not apply to

File No. I-05-227
Page 6

political advertisements placed in a newsletter put out by a fire protection district, if no
tax funds or tax-supported personnel are used in the production or distribution of the
newsletter. (Smith Advice Letter, No. A-98-117.)

You indicated that part of the funding of the newspaper's publication would be
offset by the candidates paying for advertising space. Nonetheless, you also indicated
that distribution of the newspaper is at least partially paid for with General Fund money
and that over $50 of General Fund money is spent on design, production, and printing per
issue. Therefore, it is subject to prohibition under subdivision (a)(3).

F. Over 200 Mailings Distributed

Pursuant to subdivision (a)(4) of regulation 18901, a mass mailing is subject to
regulation if "[m]ore than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single
calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request" and does
not fit an exception listed in subdivision (b). (See definitions of, e.g., "substantially
similar" [in subdivision (c)(3)] and "unsolicited request" [in subdivision (c)(4)]; see also
the subdivision (b) exceptions, described below.)

An "unsolicited request" for a mass mailing, which does not count toward the
200-mailings limit, is defined in the following five different categories contained in
regulation 18901(c)(4): (A) communications, such as petitions, requesting a response to
someone other than an elected officer or that officer's agent; (B) a request for continuing
information for a period not longer than two years; (C) a mass mailing recipient's
response to certain agency notices which indicate that, in the absence of a response, the
recipient's name will be purged from the mailing list for that mass mailing; (D) a
communication sent in response to an elected officer's participation at a public forum,
press conference, or issuance of a press release; and, (E) a request for a subscription to a
newspaper or other periodical published by non-elected officials. (Reg. 18901(c)(4)(A) –
(E).)

Pursuant to the last provision mentioned (i.e., Reg. 18901(c)(4)(E)), we have
advised that a notice of a public hearing published in a newspaper of general circulation²
that is delivered to subscribers is not a mass mailing. (Jenkins Advice Letter, A-01-213;
see Valenti Advice Letter, No. I-91-264 [prohibiting use of signature of elected city clerk
in notices of public hearings delivered to more than 200 residents in a calendar month
unless mailing is legally required and inclusion of clerk's signature is necessary to
mailing].) But since you have indicated that your newspaper is published by the City, not
sent in response to unsolicited requests, and not an adjudicated newspaper of general
circulation, none of the exemptions constituting an "unsolicited request" appear to apply.

² Government Code sections 6000 and 6008 define "newspaper of general circulation." See
Government Code sections 6001 et seq. for a fuller explanation. See also In re Norwalk Call (1964) 62
Cal.2d 185, 190 [explaining that "[t]he purpose of limiting publication [of official notices] to newspapers
meeting certain standards is to assure that the published material will come to the attention of a substantial
number of persons in the area affected. . . ."].

File No. I-05-227
Page 7

It should also be noted that copies picked up at your offices or passed out at
meetings also do not count toward the 200-item limit. (Reg. 18901(c)(4)(A); Moore
Advice Letter, No. A-93-307; Waggoner Advice Letter, No. I-89-109; Alquist Advice
Letter, No. I-89-109.)

You indicated that the newspaper is distributed by a newspaper carrier, and
through the mail, to approximately 1,100 households and businesses. Therefore, as long
as over two hundred substantially similar newspapers are sent in a single calendar month,
excluding items sent in response to unsolicited requests and any item described in
subdivision (b) of regulation 18901, the newspaper will be subject to the mass mailing
structures of the Act.

G. The Subdivision (b) Exceptions

Regulation 18901 provides a variety of exceptions to the four-criteria prohibition
described above. Those exceptions are contained in subdivision (b) of the regulation.
For instance, under subdivisions (b)(3) and (b)(4), regulation 18901 exempts inter-
governmental and intra-governmental mailings sent in the normal course of business.
Therefore, mailings which meet the criteria in subdivision (a) and which are sent in the
normal course of business to other governmental entities or officers, or to intra-agency
employees and other staff, will not be prohibited. (Reg. 18901(b)(3), (b)(4).)

To conclude, under your facts, regulation 18901 does apply to Councilmember
Solis and other candidates (who, though not council members, are elected officers
affiliated with the City), if they wish to buy political advertising space in the City's
newspaper.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Andreas C. Rockas
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

ACR:rd

It should also be noted that copies picked up at your offices or passed out at

G. The Subdivision (b) Exceptions
Regulation 18901 provides avaiety of exceptions to the four-criteria prohibition
described above. Those exceptions are contained in subdivision (b) of the regulation.
For instance, under subdivisions (b)(3) and (b)(4), regulation 18901 exemptsinter-
govemmental and intra-governmental mailings sent in the normal course of business.
Therefore, mailings which meet the criteria in subdivision (a) and which are sent in the
normal course of business to other governmental entities or officers, or to intra-agency
employees and other staff, will not be prohibited. (Reg. 18901(bX3), (bX4).)
To conclude, under your facts, regulation 18901 does apply to Councilmember
Solis and other candidates (who, though not council members, areelected officers
affiliated with the City), if they wish to buypolitical advertising space in the City,s
newspaper.

QUESTION: May Councilmember Solis, or non-incumbent candidate for that office, purchase
advertising space in the City of La Puente's community newspaper for political purposes
without implicating the mass mailing provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION: The prohibitions_rgainst publicly funded mass mailings described in the Act apply
to incumbent elected officials.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 15, 2006 Carol Cowley City Clerk City of La Puente 15900 E. Main Street La Puente, CA 91744-4719 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-05-227 Dear Ms. Cowley: This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Councilmember John Solis and the City of La Puente, regarding the mass mail provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because the facts presented are of a general nature and do not fully identify the specific facts involved in the governmental decision at issue, we are treating your request as a request for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed). Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that there are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict of interest provisions, which may apply to your situation. Laws outside the Act which may be implicated in the design and publication of the newspaper include, but are not limited to, sections 1090 et seq. regarding contracts, as well as section 8314 and Penal Code section 424 which proscribe the use of public moneys for campaign-related activities by state and local governmental entities. (See also Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal.3d 206.) _______________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All further references to statutory "sections" will be to the Government Code and all further references to "regulations" will be to title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. I-05-227 Page 2 QUESTION May Councilmember Solis, or non-incumbent candidate for that office, purchase advertising space in the City of La Puente's community newspaper for political purposes without implicating the mass mailing provisions of the Act? CONCLUSION The prohibitions against publicly funded mass mailings described in the Act apply to incumbent elected officials. FACTS The City of La Puente (the "City") publishes a free monthly community newspaper, the Spotlight, the cost of which is paid for out of the General Fund. The City does allow businesses and other individuals to buy advertising to offset the cost of printing, publishing and distributing the newspaper. The City has received a request to allow candidates to purchase space to run campaign ads in the Spotlight (the "newspaper"). In phone calls with Commission counsel on December 12 and 14, 2005, and February 7, 2006, you provided additional information regarding your question. You indicated that the requestor is Councilmember John Solis. You also
